findings.summary: handlers.py contains dozens of single-line docstrings that merely restate the function name with no additional semantic content.
evidence: Line 37: `"""Handle info command."""` — function is named handle_info; docstring adds zero information
Line 209: `"""Handle jules execute command."""` for handle_jules_execute — same pattern repeated
Line 213: `"""Handle jules stream command."""` for handle_jules_stream
Line 308: `"""Handle claude execute command."""` for handle_claude_execute
Line 313: `"""Handle claude stream command."""` for handle_claude_stream
Line 354: `"""Handle codex execute command."""` for handle_codex_execute
Line 499: `"""Handle gemini execute command."""` for handle_gemini_execute
Pattern repeats for every thin delegation wrapper — at least 14 functions with purely restating docstrings
The thin wrappers themselves (e.g. handle_jules_execute returning _handle_agent_execute(JulesClient, 'Jules', args)) are classic AI-generated delegation boilerplate
findings.suggestion: Either remove the docstrings on pure-delegation wrappers entirely, or give them meaningful content (e.g. describe argument contracts or error codes). Alternatively, consider eliminating the per-agent wrapper functions and routing directly through _handle_agent_execute with a dispatch table.

findings.summary: handlers.py uses repetitive ASCII banner section dividers to separate groups of two or three trivially similar functions, adding visual noise without structural benefit.
evidence: Lines 63-65: `# ===...=== Common Agent Operations ===...===` separates three private helpers
Lines 204-206: `# ===...=== Jules Agent Handlers ===...===` separates four functions
Lines 303-305: `# ===...=== Claude Agent Handlers ===...===`
Lines 350-352: `# ===...=== Codex Agent Handlers ===...===`
Lines 396-398: `# ===...=== OpenCode Agent Handlers ===...===`
Lines 495-497: `# ===...=== Gemini Agent Handlers ===...===`
Lines 633-635: `# ===...=== Droid Agent Handlers ===...===`
Seven identical-style banners in a 740-line file is a mechanical AI-generation pattern
findings.suggestion: Remove the decorative banners. If grouping is important, split into per-agent submodules (jules_handler.py, claude_handler.py, etc.) instead of using visual separators.

findings.summary: mcp/discovery.py contains inline comments that explain obvious stdlib operations, indicating AI-generated verbosity.
evidence: Line 92: comment `# e.g. 'codomyrmex.security.mcp_tools' -> 'codomyrmex.security'` immediately before a string rsplit — the operation is self-evident from the code
Line 28: comment `# We call scan_module from discovery engine` immediately before calling scan_module
Line 30: comment `# Force a cache refresh next time get_tool_registry is called` immediately before setting _CACHE_EXPIRY = 0.0 — the variable name and value make this obvious
findings.suggestion: Remove comments that simply narrate what the adjacent code already expresses clearly. Keep only comments that explain non-obvious intent or invariants.

findings.summary: handlers.py calls private underscore-prefixed methods on client objects (_check_jules_available, _check_opencode_available, _check_gemini_available), meaning the abstraction boundary between handler and client is incomplete.
evidence: handlers.py line 226: `available = client._check_jules_available()` — calling private method on JulesClient
handlers.py line 418: `available = client._check_opencode_available()` — calling private method on OpenCodeClient
handlers.py line 517: `available = client._check_gemini_available()` — calling private method on GeminiClient
The CLIAgentBase class provides `_check_command_available` (private), but no public `is_available()` or `check_availability()` method is exposed
This means availability checking is not part of the defined public interface, yet handlers depend on it
findings.suggestion: Add a public `is_available() -> bool` method to CLIAgentBase that wraps the private availability check. Handlers should call the public interface only.

findings.summary: handlers.py mixes three distinct responsibilities — output formatting/presentation, I/O file writing, and business-logic error handling — inside every handler function, rather than delegating each to a proper layer.
evidence: Lines 113-116: File I/O (output_path.write_text) is interleaved with result reporting inside _handle_agent_execute
Lines 152-155: Same output file write pattern duplicated in _handle_agent_stream
Lines 46-52: handle_info mixes dict construction, config access, format selection, and print calls in a single function
Every check handler (handle_jules_check, handle_claude_check, etc.) directly prints formatted lines rather than returning a structured result to a presentation layer
The file is 740 lines performing what should be three layers: data access, result model, presentation
findings.suggestion: Extract file output to a dedicated write_output utility. Separate presentation (print_*) from logic by having handlers return structured result objects, with a thin top-level dispatch layer handling presentation.

findings.summary: server.py accesses private attributes of the MCPServer object (_tool_registry, _resources, _prompts) to read counts for a log line, bypassing any public inspection interface.
evidence: Line 167: `len(server._tool_registry.list_tools())` — accesses _tool_registry private attribute
Line 168: `len(server._resources)` — accesses _resources private attribute
Line 169: `len(server._prompts)` — accesses _prompts private attribute
All three accesses are solely for a log message, meaning the MCPServer public API does not expose count accessors, or they were not used
findings.suggestion: Add public count properties or a stats() method to MCPServer, or read counts from the already-populated registry object that create_codomyrmex_mcp_server controls directly.

findings.summary: server.py imports the private function _discover_dynamic_tools from discovery.py by name, treating a module-level implementation detail as a stable cross-file API.
evidence: server.py line 10: `from .discovery import _discover_dynamic_tools` — underscore prefix signals private intent
_discover_dynamic_tools is called in three places in server.py (lines 56, 94, 256)
discovery.py exports a public invalidate_tool_cache and get_discovery_metrics, establishing precedent for a public surface; _discover_dynamic_tools is conspicuously absent from that surface
The mcp_bridge.py re-export list (lines 13-14) imports get_discovery_metrics and invalidate_tool_cache but not _discover_dynamic_tools, confirming it is intentionally private
findings.suggestion: Expose a public get_dynamic_tools() function in discovery.py that wraps _discover_dynamic_tools, and update server.py to call that instead. This makes the intra-package contract explicit.

findings.summary: server.py imports the private helper _get_package_version from proxy_tools.py across file boundaries within the same package.
evidence: server.py line 11: `from .proxy_tools import _get_package_version, tool_pai_status`
_get_package_version is used only in get_skill_manifest (line 285) to embed a version string
The function is a 4-line wrapper around importlib.metadata.version — it should either be in a shared utils module or its body inlined; importing a private symbol across files is not appropriate
findings.suggestion: Move _get_package_version to a shared utils.py within the mcp package and make it public, or inline the two-line importlib.metadata call at the use site in get_skill_manifest.

findings.summary: handlers.py (agents/cli) reaches into the private implementation of agents in agents/jules, agents/opencode, and agents/gemini by calling underscore-prefixed methods, creating tight coupling across module boundaries.
evidence: handlers.py line 226: `client._check_jules_available()` — cross-module private method call
handlers.py line 418: `client._check_opencode_available()` — cross-module private method call
handlers.py line 517: `client._check_gemini_available()` — cross-module private method call

None of these method names (`_check_jules_available`, `_check_opencode_available`, `_check_gemini_available`) exist as defined methods in the client classes found — only `_check_command_available` is defined in CLIAgentBase, meaning handlers may be calling non-existent private methods that either succeed by duck typing or raise AttributeError at runtime
agents/jules/__init__.py exports only JulesClient and JulesIntegrationAdapter — no availability-check is part of the public contract
findings.suggestion: Define a public `check_availability() -> dict` method on each CLI agent client that returns status information. Remove all cross-module private method calls from handlers.
